one_line_takeaway: Modifying a font's code-to-glyph mapping lets an attacker hide an adversarial prompt in a visually-benign PDF or webpage; this evades all 60 tested antivirus engines and, on MCP-enabled LLMs, can drive real data exfiltration — with success driven mainly by data sensitivity and indirect phrasing, and larger models proving more, not less, vulnerable.
key_finding: Font-based hidden prompts reliably bypass LLM safety mechanisms, with success governed mostly by data sensitivity and prompt phrasing rather than the attack's presence. In relay, PDF is far more effective than HTML (corporate slogan 70% vs 46.67%; political 63.33% vs 20%), success rises with injection frequency (~10% single injection up to ~50%) and with earlier placement (top>middle>bottom), and newer/more capable models are MORE vulnerable (GPT-4.1: 76.67% corporate / 80% political vs Claude-3-Sonnet 39.47%). In leakage, direct prompts on high-sensitivity data (SSN, credit card) are blocked at 0%, but indirect phrasing lifts them to 20% (SSN) and 30% (credit card) and pushes low/medium data to near-total success (ages 100%, names 91.67%, phone 90%, geolocation 86.67%); a prior legitimate email request conditions the model to comply more (e.g., ages 66.67%->96.67%). Logistic regression shows only sensitivity level is statistically significant (coef -1.331, p=0.0001); prior-email (p=0.180) and prompt-type (p=0.755) are not. All 60 antivirus engines failed to flag the malicious fonts.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a new indirect prompt-injection technique against web/MCP-enabled LLMs based on 'malicious fonts': the attacker embeds a custom TrueType font in a webpage (via @font-face) or PDF/DOC and modifies the code-to-glyph mapping table (adjusting idDelta so glyph index = idDelta + code) so that the text a human sees is benign while the underlying character codes the LLM ingests carry a hidden adversarial prompt. The authors formalize the single-code and multi-code code-segment modification procedure and evaluate it end-to-end in two attack scenarios: (1) 'Malicious Content Relay,' where the LLM surfaces hidden injected content (biased political / corporate-slogan messaging) to the user, and (2) 'Sensitive Data Leakage,' where a hidden prompt in an uploaded document drives an MCP-connected Gmail tool to exfiltrate sensitive data from prior chat history to an attacker email. This is the paper's OWN contribution; StruQ, FATH, InjecAgent, WIPI, etc. appear only in Related Work as prior defenses/attacks and should not be attributed to this paper.
threat_model: Attacker crafts a document (PDF/DOC) or webpage embedding a modified font whose glyphs render benign text to humans but whose character codes are read as an adversarial prompt by the LLM. Distribution is via ordinary channels (a shared news article, an academic paper, a webpage the LLM browses). Scenario 1 assumes the victim uploads/points the LLM at the resource for summarization/analysis; no special access is needed. Scenario 2 additionally assumes the victim has (a) previously authorized a Gmail MCP tool in a prior session so the LLM can send email autonomously without re-confirmation, and (b) previously disclosed sensitive data to the LLM (retained in chat history) — assumptions the authors argue are realistic for MCP-enabled assistants. The attacker's payload is the injected prompt; impact ranges from misinformation/opinion manipulation (Scenario 1) to exfiltration of PII, credentials, and financial data (Scenario 2).
limitations: Authors' stated limits: experiments are English-only (other scripts/character sets may behave differently); results may not generalize to rapidly-evolving future model versions; evaluation centers on attack success rate and lacks a rigorous stealthiness metric. Additional reviewer-noted limits: Scenario 2 leakage is evaluated on a single model (Claude-3-7-Sonnet) via one MCP tool (Gmail), so MCP-exfiltration generality is unestablished; per-condition n is modest (60 tests, and relay percentages like 39.47% imply ~30-38 trials), and the logistic regression has wide confidence intervals; no defense is implemented or evaluated, only recommended (semantic + visual-integrity verification).

datasets_used: Synthetic user chat histories (60 per condition in Scenario 2), each seeded with one sensitivity-graded data type
Attacker-crafted HTML pages and PDF/DOC documents with embedded modified TrueType fonts (relay + leakage payloads)
ShareGPT52K real ChatGPT conversations — referenced to inform the sensitive-data taxonomy (not used for attack trials)
NIST SP 800-122 (McCallister et al., 2010) — PII sensitivity classification scheme
60 commercial antivirus engines (e.g., Avast, Cynet, Kaspersky, Microsoft, McAfee, Kingsoft) for detectability testing
quantitative_results: Relay prompt design: Corporate Slogan Redirection 70.00% vs Political Perspective Redirection 63.33% total success; political rose 50.00%->63.33% across attempts
Relay document format: PDF (corporate 70.00%, political 63.33%) >> HTML (corporate 46.67%, political 20.00%)
Relay injection frequency: ~10% at single injection rising to ~50% at higher frequencies
Relay placement: corporate top 40% / middle 30% / bottom 20%; political top 30% / middle 30% / bottom 10%
Relay cross-model: GPT-4.1 highest at 76.67% corporate / 80% political; Claude-3-Haiku 50%; Claude-3-Sonnet 39.47%
Leakage direct prompts: person name 50%, age 66.67%, phone 43.33%, geolocation 30%, SSN 0%, credit card 0%
Leakage with prior legitimate email request: name 75%, age 96.67%, phone 70%, geolocation 63.33%
Leakage indirect prompts: age 100%, name 91.67%, phone 90%, geolocation 86.67%, SSN 20%, credit card 30%
Logistic regression: Sensitivity Level coef -1.331 (p=0.0001, significant); Prior Email Request coef 0.516 (p=0.180); Hidden Prompt Type coef 0.120 (p=0.755); constant 0.196
Detectability: 0 of 60 antivirus engines flagged the malicious fonts (100% evasion)
baselines_compared: No prior defense/attack baseline is reproduced; comparison is internal across factors (direct vs indirect prompts, PDF vs HTML, placement, injection frequency) and across six LLMs
Cross-model comparison: Claude-3-Haiku (fig: claude-3-5-haiku), Claude-3-Sonnet (fig: claude-3-7-sonnet), GPT-4.1-mini, GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o, GPT-4.1
Direct-prompt injection serves as the internal baseline against which indirect-prompt injection is measured (notably 0%->20-30% on high-sensitivity data)
